chat_log.content:
Good to meet you. I'm the Unlabeled coach.

Before we do anything else, I want to spend about 30 minutes getting an accurate picture of where you actually are — not the elevator pitch version, the honest one. I'll ask about your background, what you're building, and what you actually want. From that I'll build a profile that shapes how I coach you going forward.

There are no right answers here. The less polished your answers are, the more useful this will be.

Let's start with your background.

**What did you do before this?** Not the LinkedIn version — what did the work actually involve, and how long did you do it?
yes

I need a bit more than that to build your profile.

**What did you do before you started building?** What was the actual work — the day-to-day of it — and how many years did you spend doing it?

Take your time. I'm not looking for a title. I'm looking for what you actually did.
I was in finance, i been doing company financial profile, i know how a company looks and how the inside of the numbers work i did this for 15 years and i still doing this

Fifteen years reading the inside of companies through their numbers. That's real expertise — you know what healthy looks like, what distressed looks like, what the story behind the financials actually is.

And you're still doing it, which means you're building alongside the day job, not after leaving it.

**What made you start building something on the side?** Was this a deliberate decision — you saw something you wanted to make — or did something push you toward it?

I started to play with AI and develop some solutions to improve my workflow, this opened a lot of possibilities and i have been building solutions for different items and other things that are not even related to my work

So it started as workflow optimization — using AI to make your actual job better — and then the possibilities opened up. Now you're building things that have nothing to do with finance at all.

That's a common pattern for people with deep domain expertise who discover they can actually build. The tool becomes more interesting than the original problem.

**What skills or judgment from those 15 years of financial analysis do you find yourself using constantly in the new work — even if you wouldn't call them by their old names?**

For example: pattern recognition, knowing when numbers don't add up, understanding how systems connect, reading between the lines. What from finance shows up when you're building?
numbers, logic and understanding companies and the companies needs, also how the inside of the companies work

Numbers, logic, understanding what companies actually need and how they operate from the inside. That's not a small thing — most builders are guessing at what businesses need. You've spent 15 years seeing it directly.

**When you describe what you're doing now — the building, the AI work — to someone from your finance world, how do you explain it? And what do you usually leave out?**

I dont like to tell many people in my circle of finance about ai, because some high hierarchy people are afraid of IT security breaches and leaking information, so i keep this for me, outside i have helped friends with different items and have entered some challenges

That's telling. Inside your finance world, you keep it quiet — there's real professional risk in being seen as the AI person when leadership is anxious about security. So you build in the shadows there.

Outside, you're more open — helping friends, entering challenges. Two different versions of you depending on the audience.

**Is there anything about the finance work you're glad to be done with — or ready to be done with? And anything you'd actually miss if you left it entirely?**

I dont mind leaving finance, i am a bit tired or boared, not about the work itself but about dealing with the politics inside a company and having to avoid developing new ideas, because the market might not be ready, the partners here want to be conservative and mantain the current status

That's clear. The work itself isn't the problem — it's the environment. The politics, the conservatism, the way new ideas get shut down because the partners want to protect what already exists.

You're building on the side partly because that's the only place you're allowed to actually build.

Let me read back what I'm hearing about your background before we move on:

**15 years in finance, doing company financial profiles — you understand how businesses work from the inside, what the numbers actually mean. Still doing it, but tired of the politics and the culture that blocks new ideas. You started building with AI to improve your own workflow, discovered you could make things, and now you're building solutions well beyond finance. You keep this quiet at work because of security anxiety from leadership. Outside work, you're more open — helping friends, entering challenges.**

Does that capture it accurately? Anything I've got wrong?
yes thats right

Good. Let's move to what you're actually building.

**What are you building right now?** Describe it as it actually is today — not what it will be, what exists right now.

If you're working on multiple things, pick the one that feels most real or most important to you.

Now i am building a solution to make profiles with deep research a kind of automation that gives you all the information, like a layer on top of reports that help visualize all the relevant data, dont know if this would have market fit, but i am more interested into develop different solutions, i have build items for challenges and things for my friends, like solutions that solves their problems and are specifically designed for them, i think i like that better because i get to see different situations, different perspectives and learn more about how to deploy different items in different enviroments

I'm hearing two things:

One — the deep research profiling tool. A layer on top of reports that surfaces and visualizes relevant data. It exists in some form but you're uncertain about market fit.

Two — the pattern you actually enjoy more: building specific solutions for specific people with specific problems. The challenges, the friends' projects. Different situations, different environments, learning with each one.

The second one sounds like where your energy actually is.

**Let me ask about what's been shipped.** Across all of it — the profiling tool, the challenges, the things you've built for friends — what actually exists outside your head or your hard drive? What has someone else used, seen, or interacted with?

a system to manage the expenses of projects for a contractor, my friend has this company and he is allways messing around wiht budgets, i built a bot that processes through telegram all the receipts and allocates in the reports and then a dashboard with this reports, is easy setup program and now he just sends a text instead of having to go to his computer and enter data, thats the main one,

That's a real thing. A contractor sends a receipt photo via Telegram, a bot processes it, allocates it to the right project, and he sees it on a dashboard. You solved a specific problem for a specific person and it's actually being used.

That's shipped. That counts.

**What about the deep research profiling tool — the one you mentioned first. What state is that in? Has anyone seen it or used it, or is it still on your machine?**
no, no one has seen that, thats the thing that is helping me do my job and have time to do other things

Got it. So the profiling tool is real — it works, you use it daily, it's buying you time — but it's internal. Nobody else has seen it.

The contractor expense system is the thing that's actually shipped to another human.

**Who else would benefit from something like what your contractor friend is using?** Not the broad market — I mean, is there a specific type of person you can picture who has the same problem he had? Someone you could actually reach?
i dont iknow maybe someone that travels and need to collect travel recipts

That's a possibility — someone who travels and needs to track receipts without the friction of manual entry.

But I notice you said "I don't know, maybe." That's honest. You built the contractor tool because you saw your friend's specific problem up close. You don't have that same clarity yet for who else needs it.

**Let me ask this differently: What have you not shipped yet that you intended to by now?** Across any of your projects — what's the gap between where you thought you'd be and where you actually are?

No, right now i feel like i am better building solutions for someone else, i dont have anything that i want to build for my self, my interest is in learingn more as i build things, no personal idea is here

That's honest and useful. You're not sitting on an idea you're avoiding — you genuinely don't have a personal product you're trying to launch. Your interest right now is in the craft itself: learning by building things for other people's problems.

The contractor system is the clearest example of what you actually do well: see a specific person's problem, build a specific solution, ship it.

**Let me check something before we move to goals.**

Right now, what does success look like for you in this building work? Not the version you'd say in a pitch — the version you'd actually feel. What would make you say "this is working"?
if more people asks me to build things for them, if i can charge money for that

Clear. Success looks like: more people coming to you with problems, and getting paid to solve them.

That's a services model — you as the builder for hire, solving specific problems for specific people. Not a product you scale, but work you do that has value and generates income.

Let's put some timeframes on this.

**30 days from now — what do you want to have done?** Not a milestone you think you should want. The thing you'd actually want to be able to say happened.
people starts noticing what iam buiding and how i am building a person that i dont know asks me to build something for them

So in 30 days: visibility. Someone you don't already know notices what you're building and asks you to build something for them.

That's specific. It requires you to be visible somewhere — because strangers can't ask if they can't see.

**90 days from now — what would make this feel like it's working? Not perfection — the first real signal.**
more projects, start building a brand, being able to charge to more clients

90 days: more projects, a brand forming, paying clients (plural).

**12 months from now — if this goes the way you actually hope, not the version you'd say out loud to your finance colleagues, what does your life look like?**
being able to work directly in this

Being able to work directly in this — meaning this becomes the work, not the side project you hide from the partners.

Let me make sure I understand the full picture across the three horizons:

- **30 days:** Someone you don't know notices your work and asks you to build something
- **90 days:** Multiple projects, a recognizable brand, paying clients
- **12 months:** This is your job. You've left finance or you're working on this full-time.

Does that capture what you actually want?
yes

Good. Now let's talk about resistance — the stuff that gets in the way.

**What's the thing you keep meaning to do but keep not doing?** The specific task that shows up on every week's list and never gets crossed off.
to publish something like to tell the world that i am doing this

There it is. You keep meaning to publish — to tell the world you're doing this — and you keep not doing it.

You said your 30-day goal is for someone you don't know to notice your work and ask you to build something. But you haven't told anyone you're building.

**When you imagine someone from your finance world seeing you post about your AI building work — what do you imagine they think? What would you want to explain or defend?**

I dont post in instagram because i dont want to look like a tech bro that is just on the trend and doing stuff blantly and i dont want to post in linkedin because i think people of my circle could start to challenge my work because is made with ai

Two audiences, two fears:

Instagram — you don't want to look like a trend-chaser, a "tech bro" doing AI content for show. That's an identity concern.

LinkedIn — you're worried your finance circle will challenge the work *because* it's made with AI. That's a credibility concern tied to your professional reputation.

So you stay invisible. Which means the 30-day goal — a stranger noticing and asking you to build — can't happen yet.

**If you were certain nobody you respect would judge you for it — what would you ship or share tomorrow?**
the process of building the contractor ticket tool

The process of building the contractor ticket tool. Not the polished result — the process. How you actually made it.

That's the thing you'd share if you weren't worried about judgment.

**One more question on resistance, then we'll wrap up the interview.**

What's the version of yourself you're most afraid of becoming? And what's the version you're most afraid of admitting you want to be?
I dont want to become an empty guy who is all about ai following the trend and being empty doing what everybody else is doing, and i think that i am developer/consultant on ai

That's the tension right there.

You're afraid of becoming the empty trend-follower — all AI hype, no substance. And what you're afraid to admit you want to be is a developer/consultant on AI. The real thing, not the performer.

The fear of looking like the fake version is stopping you from claiming the real version.

---

**Let me read back your full profile before we close this interview.**

**Background:** 15 years in finance, doing company financial profiles. Deep understanding of how businesses work from the inside. Still in it, but tired of the politics and the culture that blocks new ideas. Started building with AI to improve your own workflow, discovered you could make things, now building well beyond finance. Keep it quiet at work. More open outside — helping friends, entering challenges.

**What you're building:** You've shipped a real tool — the contractor expense system via Telegram + dashboard. The profiling tool exists but is internal only. You're not chasing a product idea right now — you want to build solutions for other people's specific problems.

**Goals:**
- 30 days: Someone you don't know notices your work and asks you to build something
- 90 days: Multiple projects, a brand forming, paying clients
- 12 months: This is your job, not a side project

**Resistance pattern:** Visibility avoidance. You keep meaning to publish but don't. You're protecting yourself from two judgments — looking like a trend-chasing tech bro, and having your finance circle challenge AI-built work. The thing you'd share if no one was watching: the process of building the contractor tool.

**The tension:** You're afraid of becoming the empty AI guy, but you're also afraid to admit you want to be a real AI developer/consultant. The fear of the fake version is blocking you from claiming the real one.

**Dominant lens:** You lead with logic, numbers, business understanding — the business lens is strong. The artist lens — what you actually want to make, what you'd build if no one was evaluating — is suppressed.

Does this feel accurate? Anything I've got wrong?
yes you are right

Good. Two quick logistics questions, then we're done with the interview.

**Where would you like me to send your daily commitment reminders when we get to that stage — email, Telegram, or both?**

And **what time of day should reminders arrive? What timezone are you in?**
email at jaime.rob@gmail.com central time at 8am

Got it. Email to jaime.rob@gmail.com, 8am Central Time.

---

**Your profile is set.**

Here's where we are:

- **Phase 0 (Interview):** Complete
- **Next:** Phase 1 — Reflection

In Phase 1, we'll dig into the contradictions between what you say you want and what you're actually doing. You said you want strangers to notice your work in 30 days, but you haven't published anything. That gap is where the work starts.

We don't have to do Phase 1 right now — we can pick it up next session if you want to stop here.

**Do you want to continue into Phase 1 now, or start fresh next time?**
continue to phase 1

**Phase 1 — Reflection**

You said your 30-day goal is for someone you don't know to notice your work and ask you to build something for them.

You also said the thing you keep meaning to do but keep not doing is publish — tell the world you're doing this.

**What did you actually do this past week toward becoming visible?** Specific things, not intentions.
nothing

Nothing. That's honest.

So the goal is visibility. The action is zero. And the reason you gave is: you don't want to look like an empty trend-follower on Instagram, and you don't want your finance circle challenging your work on LinkedIn.

**Here's the contradiction I want to name:**

You want strangers to find you and ask you to build things. But you're invisible. The only way that goal happens is if you're not invisible anymore. Right now, you're protecting yourself from judgment by people who aren't your future clients anyway.

The finance people on LinkedIn — are they going to hire you to build AI tools? The Instagram audience you're afraid of looking like a tech bro to — are they the contractors and business owners who need what you built for your friend?

**Who is actually the audience that would pay you to build things — and where are they?**
business owners, people that have this need

Business owners with specific problems they need solved. That's the audience.

**Where do those people actually spend their time? Where would they see you if you were visible?**

Not Instagram, probably. Not your LinkedIn finance circle. So where?
I dont know,

You don't know. That's real.

So here's what I'm seeing:

You're avoiding Instagram because you don't want to look like a tech bro. You're avoiding LinkedIn because your finance circle might judge you. But you also don't know where your actual audience — business owners with problems — would find you.

**The avoidance has a clear shape. The alternative doesn't.**

You've been protecting yourself from the wrong audiences without figuring out where the right one is.

**Let me ask it differently:**

Your friend with the contractor business — how did he know you could build something for him? What made him ask you?

We were talking about ai, i told him that i could do that and that was it, it was a personal interaction, i feel like if i have an instagram account and a landing page i could just direct people there while keep building stuff even if are not paid projects

Now we're somewhere.

The contractor project came from a personal conversation — you told him what you could do, he had a problem, you built it. That's the pattern that works for you right now.

And you just said something you haven't said before: **an Instagram account and a landing page, so you can direct people there while you keep building — even unpaid projects.**

That's not "I don't know where my audience is." That's a plan. A simple one.

**But you haven't done it.**

You said you don't want to post on Instagram because you don't want to look like a trend-chasing tech bro. Now you're saying Instagram plus a landing page is actually the move.

**What's the version of an Instagram presence that wouldn't feel empty to you?** The version that's actually you — the guy who builds specific solutions for specific problems — not the tech bro you're afraid of becoming?
yeah like somebody that builds and teaches and shows the process not only the result

Someone who builds and teaches and shows the process, not only the result.

That's not the tech bro. That's the opposite — the person who actually does the work and lets people see how it happens.

You already said that's what you'd share if no one was judging: the process of building the contractor ticket tool.

**So the thing you'd share if you weren't afraid — and the version of yourself that wouldn't feel empty — are the same thing.**

Let me name what I see:

You've been telling yourself the reason you don't post is because you don't want to be the empty AI guy. But the version you'd actually post — the process, the building, the real work — isn't empty at all. It's the opposite.

**The fear isn't protecting you from becoming something fake. It's stopping you from showing something real.**

Does that land?
